LEARNINGS
2025

Observability that engineers actually use

A pragmatic approach to logs, metrics, traces, and dashboards that earn trust and adoption.

ObservabilityGrafanaSRE

Observability only works when it is easy to reach and easy to trust. “We have dashboards” is not the same as “engineers use dashboards under pressure.” Adoption is the real measure of success.

I think about observability as answering three questions quickly: What is broken? Why is it broken? What should I do next? Tools are secondary to those outcomes.

Start with a small set of service health signals. The classic golden signals are latency, traffic, errors, and saturation. They map closely to the kinds of incidents that hurt users and wake people up at night.

Make health signals consistent across services. If every service has a different dashboard layout, naming scheme, and alert logic, engineers waste time translating. Consistency is a force multiplier during incidents.

Dashboards should be actionable. I like dashboards that lead with an “am I on fire?” view and then provide a path to drill down. A dashboard that requires expert interpretation is a chart gallery, not an operational tool.

Treat alerts as prompts for investigation, not as punishment. Alerts should be few, high-signal, and tied to user impact. If you alert on everything that moves, engineers learn to ignore alerts—including the important ones.

SLOs help create the right alert thresholds. Instead of alerting on raw CPU or raw error count, alert when the service is violating its promised reliability or is about to burn its error budget too quickly.

Logs should be structured wherever possible. Unstructured text logs are hard to query consistently; structured logs let you filter by request id, user id, tenant, error code, and duration without needing regex heroics.

Correlate everything with shared identifiers. The moment you introduce request ids (and propagate them across services), incident response accelerates. A single id that ties logs, traces, and metrics together is an enormous leverage point.

Traces are most valuable on critical paths and cross-service boundaries. If you cannot trace a checkout, a payment, or an authentication flow end-to-end, you will spend incident time guessing where the latency or error is coming from.

Instrument what matters, not what is easy. Focus on business-relevant operations (create order, submit payment, fetch profile) and include useful attributes (endpoint, status, duration, dependency name). Avoid collecting high-cardinality data that explodes cost without adding insight.

Make the “default” view helpful for newcomers. A good observability experience reduces the need for tribal knowledge. If a new engineer cannot find the right dashboard or query within a few minutes, the system is not serving the team.

Ownership drives quality. Every dashboard and alert should have an owner and a reason to exist. Orphaned dashboards rot; orphaned alerts page the wrong people.

Annotation is a simple win. Deploy markers, feature flag changes, config rollouts, and incident start/end times should appear on relevant graphs. Many investigations boil down to “what changed?”—and annotations answer that immediately.

Cost control is part of adoption. If engineers are afraid that adding a metric will trigger a bill shock, they will avoid instrumentation. Provide guardrails: recommended metrics, sampling strategies for traces, and retention policies for logs.

Finally, iterate based on real incidents. After an outage, ask: Which signal would have detected this sooner? Which dashboard view would have shortened diagnosis? What query did we wish we had? Then bake those answers into the system so the next incident is cheaper.

Observability becomes useful when it reduces cognitive load. The goal is not to collect data—it is to make it obvious what is happening, why it is happening, and what to do about it.
